column.name: title
column.value: The busker who fills the square every Friday night

column.name: summary
column.value:
Every Friday at dusk, a crowd three-deep gathers on the market square for a musician the city never booked. He arrives with a battered amp and a loop pedal, and for two hours the square belongs to him — takeaway coffees go cold as people stop, film, and stay.
The council is quietly uneasy. Traders love the footfall but complain about the noise; residents above the shops are split between charmed and exhausted. A proposed licensing scheme would cap performance hours and require an audition — supporters call it fair, buskers call it the end of the thing that makes a street feel alive.
It is a small fight about a big question: who gets to decide what a public space sounds like, and whether the unplanned moments are a nuisance to manage or the whole point.

column.name: support_question
column.value: Should street performers need a licence to play in public spaces?

column.name: case_for
column.value: Licensing caps noise, protects residents and keeps pitches fairly shared.

column.name: case_against
column.value: Auditions and permits kill the spontaneity that makes a street feel alive.
